***Project Title:*** The Neural Gap  
***Student Name:*** Maksim Kniazev  
***Date:*** 1/6/2026  
***Course:*** Software Engineering Stage 6  
***GitHub URL (if applicable):*** [https://github.com/maksimK12-ux/The-Neural-Gap](https://github.com/maksimK12-ux/The-Neural-Gap)

***1\. Identifying and Defining***  
**1.1 Problem Statement**  
*Outline the problem or opportunity that the project addresses. Consider who is affected*  
*by this issue:* This project targets users who require a good reaction time in their daily lives or professions. Having a good reaction time is crucial for maintaining the speed of an individual's nervous system, brain processing and motor response. This project could also be used in a professional setting to measure the cognitive health of an individual and determine whether brain injuries impact reaction time. A reaction time test also determines if individuals in high-stakes jobs are worthy of having them by being alert and ready.

*Explain why this problem or opportunity is significant:* This is significant because good reaction time is critical as it enhances safety by allowing individuals to avoid accidents (braking in time, catching a fall). It is also essential for high-level athletic and gaming performances. It also reflects overall brain function and health.

*Justify the development of a software solution as an appropriate response:* A software solution would be justified because it would develop a high quality game that appeals to many users and be functional, accessible and high quality.

**1.2 Project Purpose and Boundaries**  
*Outline what the project is trying to achieve:* This project aims to measure the speed at which someone's central nervous system processes sensory input and produces a corresponding motor response in the form of a reaction time test. It measures how quickly a person can perceive an auditory or visual signal, typically in milliseconds.

**1.3 Stakeholder Requirements**  
*Identify stakeholders (client, users, teacher, peers):* Athletes, Gamers, Doctors, The average person, Pilots  
*Describe their needs, expectations, and how these influenced the project direction:* Some needs of the stakeholders are to measure their reaction time based on a specific sense (for example, pilots need good visual reaction time to steer clear of obstacles). The expectations are for this test to be accurate, work properly and save your results to track if your reaction time is changing for the better or worse.

**1.4 Functional Requirements**  
*List and describe what the system must do:* The system must function properly and give out accurate results based on the test that is being done by the user. It must measure the user's reaction time in a variety of tests made for different senses. The system must also save the users results to determine if their reaction time changes.

**1.5 Non-Functional Requirements**  
*List and describe system qualities such as:*  
*Performance:* The performance must be fast and accurate for the user, allowing for better navigation and easier results. The performance must stay the same no matter where the user is or which engine they are using.  
*Usability:* The program needs to be accessible and reliable for the user. It should also be easy to use and navigate for everyone, no matter their age, gender or possible disability.   
*Security:* The program must be trustworthy and safe, protecting the users information and statistics.  
*Reliability:* The program must display accurate results so that it is reliable for the user.

**1.6 Constraints**  
*Identify limitations affecting the project, such as:*  
*Time:* There is a lot of time to do the project and if I work efficiently, time shouldn’t be a problem throughout the creation of this project.  
*Technical knowledge:* Python and tkinter are not new to me so the base technical knowledge is there but during the project, I may experiment with new aspects of python. JSON is also not new to me, making me familiar with everything I’m using.  
*Hardware/software access*: I have access to all the hardware/software that I will need for this project so that shouldn’t be a problem.

**1.7 Requirements Analysis and Prioritisation**  
*Analyse the functional and non-functional requirements. In your analysis, consider:*  
*Which requirements were prioritised and why:* I prioritised usability and performance because they transform the code into an experience for users to enjoy and feel safe with. Performance ensures the system is fast, responsive, and reliable, while usability ensures it is intuitive, easy to learn, and efficient to navigate.  
*Trade-offs made due to constraints:* Focusing on security is quite unrealistic as it would take a lot of time to incorporate security measures, more time than I have allocated to finish this task to a good extent.   
*How requirements align with the identified problem or opportunity:* My requirements align with my identified problems because to efficiently provide accurate results for people wanting to test their reaction time, the program must have a good, accessible usability and must be reliable and precise.

***2\. Research and Planning***  
**2.1 Development Methodology**  
*Describe the development approach used (e.g. Agile, Waterfall, WAgile):* The development approach of this project is Waterfall as it is a step-by-step approach that requires each stage to be completed before the next one begins.

*Justify the suitability of this methodology.*  
*Project size and complexity:* This development approach suits the size and complexity of my project because the requirements are set out effectively and clearly.  
*Time constraints:* Time constraints shouldn’t impact me because I have laid out clear requirements and the code and design are going to be done in steps, minimising the amount of time it’ll take me to complete the task.  
*Feedback and iteration requirements:* This method is suitable for feedback and iteration requirements because it is very simple and easy to understand. It appeals to users and is very accessible, it has a very understandable UI and usability, making it prone to good feedback from users.

**2.2 Tools and Technologies**  
*Justify the selection of software applications, engines, developer tools, programming*  
*languages, IDEs, frameworks, libraries and/or hardware components:* For this project I am using Python in Visual Studio Code, I will also use Tkinter to make windows that will display and run my program. I am using Python because I believe that it is the most suitable for my code as it accommodates Tkinter and has simple syntax, which is optimal for fast and efficient development. I am using JSON to store data as it is a reliable aspect of my project.

*Explain how these tools supported efficient and effective development:*   
The tools used in this project support a faster and more efficient development process. Python's simple syntax makes the code easier to write and understand, while Visual Studio Code provides useful features such as debugging and code suggestions that help identify and fix errors. Tkinter makes it easy to create the graphical user interface, and JSON provides a simple and reliable way to store user accounts and test results. Together, these tools help produce a functional, reliable, and user-friendly software solution.

**2.3 Gantt Chart / Timeline**  
*Include a timeline showing key project milestones.*  
*Explain how time was allocated to planning, development, testing, and evaluation.*  
![][image1]  
For me, I allocated time which didn’t interfere with any other schoolwork and didn’t affect my personal time. This is why for a part of the holidays, I hadn’t started producing and implementing my program. I didn’t allocate much planning time since it didn’t take me that long to think of an idea, for this I allowed about a week. For development, I allowed the most time as my task is quite large and contains many variables which took time to be implemented properly. For testing, I didn’t need that much time as I simply asked my peers for feedback and built upon it to improve my code. For the evaluation, I spent quality time reviewing my program.

**2.4 Communication Plan**  
*Explain how client or peer feedback was obtained and incorporated.*  
To obtain peer feedback, I asked my friends to review the code and try running it. After they did, I was provided with feedback to highlight my good assets and what I could work on. I listened to the constructive criticism and made tweaks to the code that improved the site.

**2.5 Resource Allocation Justification**  
*Justify the resource allocation for the project, including:*  
*Time:* Time allocation was a crucial part of the project as it allowed me to focus on it at optimal times and manage my time to the fullest while maintaining sufficient progress  
*Software and hardware tools:* I needed to use Python and tkinter in VS Code, this made up the foundation of my project and allowed me to create it in the first place. I also used JSON to store user results and information for future use.  
*Human input (client, peers, teacher feedback):* Human input played an important role in the development of my project as it allowed me to remove weaknesses from my code and optimise it without complaints.

# ***3\. System Design***

**3.1 Context Diagram**  
![][image2]

**3.2 Data Flow Diagrams (Level 0 and Level 1\)**  
![][image3]![][image4]

**3.3 Structure Chart**

![][image5]

**3.4 IPO Chart**

| Input | Process | Output |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| User interaction  | Detect stimulus | Reaction time displayed in milliseconds |
| Visual or auditory stimulus trigger | Start timer when stimulus appears | Immediate feedback to user |
| Login | Validation of user credentials | Access to user information |
| User data | Calculate reaction duration | Stored results for tracking progress |
| System time | Record timestamps for start and response | Reliable and precise measurements |
| Previous reaction time results | Compare current result with past data | Performance trends |

**3.5 Data Dictionary**

| Name | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| username | string | Stores the name or ID of the user |
| test type | string | Identifies the type of reaction test selected |
| reaction time | float/integer | Records the time when the user reacts in milliseconds |
| results list | list | Stores multiple reaction time results for a user session. |
| average time | float/integer | Calculates the average reaction time from results list |
| user input | string | Captures the user's input action. |
| save | JSON | Stores saved user information and data |

**3.6 UML Class Diagram (if OOP)**  
*Explain the class structure and relationships.*  
The structure of the classes makes it so that they all connect and are being used within themselves. The user must first register and make an account before logging in, after this, the user accesses the UI to the reaction test and is given the option between different kinds before settling on one and recording results. After the results are recorded, they are sent to the results manager which saves them to the JSONHandler which stores them for future use.   
![][image6]

# ***4\. Producing and implementing***

**4.1 Development Process**  
*Describe how the solution was built and implemented.*  
My project was developed using Python and the Tkinter library to create an interactive graphical user interface that allows users to complete a range of cognitive performance tests. It was built using a modular design approach, where different components of the program were separated into individual files responsible for authentication, data management, testing systems, and analytics. Users can log in securely via the registration system which uses hashing and salting techniques to store passwords in JSON files. My project includes 3 different reaction time tests, each designed to measure different aspects of cognitive performance. After the user completes the tests, their results are saved through JSON storage which allows the user's progress to be tracked over time. Throughout development, testing was conducted to ensure functionality, reliability, and accuracy across all features of the system.

*Justify the engineering techniques used*  
Several software engineering techniques were used to improve the quality and reliability of my project. A modular design approach was used by separating the program into multiple files, making the code easier to develop, test, and maintain. Object-oriented principles were applied through the use of classes to organise related data and functionality, improving code structure and reusability. Code reuse was achieved through functions that could be called multiple times throughout the program, reducing duplication and increasing efficiency. Validation and error handling were also implemented to ensure users entered valid information and to prevent the program from crashing when unexpected errors occurred. These techniques helped create a more secure, maintainable, and user-friendly software solution.

**4.2 Key Features Developed**  
*Describe the core features of the system.*  
My project includes several core features designed to measure and analyse cognitive performance. Users can create secure accounts, log in, and have their results saved automatically for future sessions. Results are stored in a JSON database and can be viewed through a statistics dashboard that provides averages, personal bests, progress graphs, and a reaction age analysis. These features work together to give users meaningful feedback on their performance over time. The user-friendly interface also makes the system easy to navigate and accessible to a wide range of users.  
*Justify their inclusion.*  
The core features were included to ensure the software effectively met its purpose of measuring and analysing cognitive performance. The secure login system protects user data and allows results to be stored and retrieved across multiple sessions. The reaction time, sequence memory, and aim trainer tests were included because they assess different aspects of cognitive ability, providing a more comprehensive evaluation of performance. The statistics dashboard, progress graphs, and reaction age analysis were added to help users interpret their results and track improvement over time. Together, these features make the system more useful, informative, and engaging for users.

**4.2.1 Back-End Engineering Contribution**  
*Explain how back-end engineering contributed to the success and ease of use of the*  
*Software*  
Back-end engineering contributed significantly to the success and ease of use of The Neural Gap by managing the system's data processing, validation, storage, and authentication. Data processing was used to calculate reaction times, averages, personal bests, and reaction age estimates, providing users with meaningful performance feedback. Validation and program logic ensured that user inputs were correct and prevented errors such as invalid login details or incomplete account information. Storage and retrieval were handled through JSON files, allowing user accounts and test results to be saved and accessed across multiple sessions. Authentication was implemented through a secure login and registration system, with passwords protected using hashing and salting techniques.

# ***5\. Testing and evaluating***

**5.1 Testing Methods Used**  
*Describe testing approaches.*  
I used several testing to ensure my project functioned reliably and met its intended requirements accurately and effectively. Unit testing was done by testing individual components, such as the login system, reaction time calculations, and JSON data storage, to confirm each feature worked correctly on its own. Integration testing was then used to make sure that all modules, including authentication, cognitive tests and analytics, worked together seamlessly and shared information correctly. User testing involved interacting with the software as a user to assess the usability of the interface, the responsiveness of the controls, and the accuracy of the results. These testing approaches helped identify and resolve issues, resulting in a more stable, reliable, and user-friendly application.

*Explain how testing results were used to improve performance, efficiency, or reliability.*  
Testing results were used to identify bugs, improve system performance, and increase the overall reliability of the software. Errors found while testing, such as issues with user authentication and data storage, were corrected to ensure the program functioned as intended. Testing also helped optimise the efficiency of the code by refining program logic and reducing unnecessary processing. Feedback from users was used to improve the UI, making the software easier to navigate and easier to use. As a result, the final system became more stable, efficient, and reliable while providing a better user experience.  
**5.2 Test Cases and Results**

| Test ID | Description | Expected Result | Actual Result | Pass/Fail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TC01 | Register username Maksim289 with password mnk4952 | A new account is created | Account created and stored successfully | Pass |
| TC02 | Register username Maksim again | Registration is rejected | Error message displayed stating username already exists | Pass |
| TC03 | Login with username Maksim289 and password mnk4952 | User logs into the main menu | Login successful | Pass |
| TC04 | Login with username Maksim289 and password ilovese1 | Login denied | Error message displayed | Pass |
| TC05 | Login with username Maksim111 and password mnk4952 | User account does not exist | Error message displayed | Pass |
| TC06 | Complete a reaction time test | Average reaction time is saved | Result saved correctly to JSON | Pass |
| TC07 | Complete a Sequence Memory test | Final level is saved to user account | Score saved correctly | Pass |
| TC08 | Complete and Aim Trainer test | Average aim time is calculated and saved | Result saved correctly | Pass |
| TC09 | View Statistics Dashboard | User statistics are displayed | Dashboard displayed averages and personal bests | Pass |
| TC10 | Generate a Progress Graph after multiple reaction tests | Graph displays previous reaction times | Graph generated successfully | Pass |
| TC11 | Export results to a JSON file | User data exported successfully | Export completed successfully | Pass |
| TC12 | Register using an empty username or password | Registration should be prevented | Validation message displayed and registration blocked | Pass |
| TC13 | Enter a username containing only symbols | Username should be rejected | Username was accepted | Fail |

**5.3 Evaluation Against Requirements**  
*Evaluate how effectively the solution meets the identified functional and non-functional*  
*requirements. Consider your ongoing quality assurance processes.*  
The Neural Gap effectively meets the majority of its functional and non-functional requirements. Functionally, the software allows users to create accounts, securely log in, complete three cognitive tests, save their results, and view performance statistics and progress over time. The prioritised non-functional requirements of usability and performance were also achieved through a simple user interface, responsive controls, and data storage using JSON files. Ongoing quality assurance processes, such as unit, integration, and user testing, helped identify and correct issues relating to authentication, data handling and the tests themselves, improving the reliability and usability of the final product. Due to time constraints, some advanced security features were not implemented, however, password hashing and validation still provide an appropriate level of protection for the project's scope. Overall, the final result successfully addresses the original problem by providing an accurate, reliable, and user-friendly system for measuring and tracking cognitive performance.

*Evaluate your project in terms of how effectively you addressed compliance and*  
*legislative requirements (consider privacy, use of data, etc).*  
My project successfully addressed the main compliance and legislative considerations relevant to the project, particularly in relation to user privacy and data security. Only the information required for the software to function, such as usernames, encrypted passwords, and test results, was collected and stored. Passwords were protected using hashing and salting techniques rather than being saved in plain text, reducing the risk of unauthorised access. User data was stored locally in JSON files instead of being transmitted online, helping maintain user privacy. The project also relied on Python libraries, ensuring software was used appropriately. Although the application does not implement advanced security measures required for advanced software, it demonstrates a high consideration of privacy, ethical data handling, and security.

**5.4 Improvements and Future Development**  
*Outline your project’s limitations.*  
The Neural Gap successfully achieves its intended purpose, although there are some areas for improvement. User data is currently stored locally using JSON files, so accounts are only available on the device they were created on. The software includes a range of cognitive tests and performance statistics, but additional assessments and more detailed analytics could further enhance the user experience. Overall, these limitations do not significantly affect the functionality or reliability of the software.  
*Explain realistic future enhancements.*  
Future enhancements could improve both the functionality and accessibility of The Neural Gap. User accounts could be connected to a secure database, allowing access across multiple devices while maintaining data security. Additional cognitive tests and more detailed performance analytics could provide users with greater insight into their results. The graphical user interface could also be further refined with additional customisation options to create a more engaging user experience.

**5.5 Evaluation of Social, Ethical and Communication Issues**  
*Evaluate your project in terms of social, ethical and legal issues*  
The Neural Gap demonstrates consideration of important social, ethical, and legal issues that are relevant to software development. From a social perspective, the application provides users with an accessible way to measure and track their cognitive performance, helping them better understand their reaction time, memory, and coordination. Ethically, the software protects user privacy by only collecting the information needed for the program to function and storing passwords securely using hashing and salting instead of plain text. The software also displays results as performance indicators rather than medical advice, ensuring users are not given misleading information. From a legal perspective, user data is stored locally on the user's device, reducing privacy concerns, and the project uses legitimate programming libraries and software. Overall, the project effectively addresses these social, ethical, and legal considerations while remaining reliable and usable for all users.

***6\. Feedback, Security and Reflection***  
**6.1 Summary of Client or Peer Feedback**  
*Summarise feedback received and explain how it influenced development.*

|  | Plus | Minus | Implication |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Ario | Enjoyed testing, fun to use, good login system | Results seemed off/inaccurate, could be better with more features | It can lead people into thinking that they’re slower than they really are. |
| Jamie | Enjoyed testing, statistics displayed well, clear objective | Not enough security, could have more options for tests | Two factor authentication would make this a lot better |
| Rufus | Secure registration, secure storage of data, fun tests | Could look a bit better, more test options, more accurate results | If more tests are implemented, users will be more attracted to the project. |

User feedback was largely positive, with testers enjoying the cognitive tests, clear interface, secure login system, and informative statistics. However, they also suggested improvements such as enhancing the visual design, adding more test options, strengthening security with features like two-factor authentication, and improving the accuracy of the results. This feedback influenced the evaluation of the project by identifying practical areas for future development, helping to improve the software's functionality, security, and overall user experience.

*Evaluate your use of feedback to improve your project:*  
Feedback played an important role in evaluating and improving the project throughout its development. Suggestions from peers and stakeholders were carefully considered and used to identify strengths as well as areas for improvement, such as expanding the range of cognitive tests, refining the user interface, and strengthening security features. Maintaining regular communication with testers allowed their opinions to influence design decisions and ensured the software continued to meet user expectations. Responding to this feedback helped produce a more user-friendly, reliable, and effective final solution while demonstrating an ability to collaborate and adapt throughout the development process.

**6.2 Secure Software Design and Data Handling**  
*Evaluate the approach undertaken to safely and securely collect, use, and store data.*  
Secure software engineering principles were considered throughout the design and implementation of the project to ensure user data was handled responsibly. Appropriate coding practices were applied to reduce security risks, including protecting passwords through hashing and salting rather than storing them in plain text. Input validation and error handling were incorporated to verify user information, minimise invalid inputs, and improve the stability of the application by preventing unexpected errors. Data was stored locally using JSON files, providing a practical and reliable solution for managing user accounts and performance results. These measures helped maintain data integrity by ensuring information remained accurate and consistent, while also contributing to the overall reliability of the software. The approach taken demonstrates an understanding of secure software design and its importance in protecting user information, promoting user trust, and supporting the functionality of the project.

**6.3 Personal Reflection**  
This project allowed me to significantly develop my software engineering skills, particularly in Python programming, Tkinter UI development, and JSON data management. Throughout the project, I gained a deeper understanding of creating interactive user interfaces, securely storing and retrieving user data, and organising code using a modular structure to improve readability and maintenance. One of the biggest challenges I encountered was errors in code when different parts of the program did not work together as expected. I overcame these issues by testing individual components, identifying the source of problems, and altering the code until the software functioned correctly and reliably. Another challenge was managing my time effectively, particularly when unexpected bugs delayed progress. By prioritising the most important features, planning my workload, and consistently testing the software, I was able to overcome these challenges and produce a functional, reliable, and user-friendly final solution.
